name: Broadcom Inc.
shortDescription: Designs custom AI/networking silicon and runs infrastructure software including VMware for hyperscalers and enterprises.
businessModel: Two segments: Semiconductor Solutions (58% of FY2025 revenue, $36.9B) spanning custom AI accelerators (XPUs), AI networking, broadband, wireless, and storage; and Infrastructure Software (42%, $27.0B) led by VMware. Broadcom monetizes silicon through custom ASIC/XPU design wins and merchant networking chips, and software through multi-year subscriptions.
marketContext: Broadcom is the leading merchant supplier of custom AI accelerators (XPUs) and AI-networking silicon, the primary alternative to Nvidia's GPUs for hyperscalers building in-house chips. It competes with Marvell in custom ASICs/optical, with Qualcomm in wireless, and leads Ethernet-based AI fabric switching (Tomahawk/Jericho) against Nvidia's InfiniBand/Spectrum.

explainer.plain: Broadcom makes two things. First, specialized computer chips: the custom processors and networking gear that run AI data centers for giants like Google, Meta and OpenAI. Second, enterprise software: it owns VMware, which runs the behind-the-scenes infrastructure of corporate data centers. Broadcom does not sell to consumers; its customers are the world's largest technology and enterprise companies.
explainer.technical: Broadcom is a fabless designer of custom ASICs (it calls them XPUs) and merchant networking silicon, all built at TSMC. For hyperscalers it co-designs application-specific AI accelerators (for example Google's TPU and Meta's MTIA) using its in-house SerDes, advanced packaging and IP. It also supplies the AI cluster fabric: high-radix Ethernet switches (Tomahawk) and routers (Jericho) plus optical interconnect, competing with Nvidia's InfiniBand and Spectrum. The software segment monetizes VMware Cloud Foundation through multi-year subscriptions after re-architecting licensing post-acquisition. FY2025 revenue is roughly 58% semiconductors and 42% software.
explainer.simple: Picture the AI boom as a gold rush. Nvidia sells the most popular shovels, the GPUs anyone can buy off the shelf. Broadcom is the company that, if you are a giant like Google, will build you a custom shovel designed for exactly your dirt, usually cheaper and more power-efficient than the standard one. Broadcom also lays the roads and pipes that connect thousands of those shovels so they work as one giant machine. And on the side it owns VMware, the software big companies use to run their own computer rooms. So Broadcom earns money two ways: building custom AI chips plus the plumbing between them, and renting essential software to large enterprises.

differentiation.theyMake: One-size-fits-all merchant GPUs anyone can buy
differentiation.avgoEdge: Broadcom co-designs a chip for a single customer's workload, usually cheaper and lower-power for that job, and owns the Ethernet networking layer Nvidia tries to capture with InfiniBand.

differentiation.theyMake: Custom ASICs plus optical and networking silicon
differentiation.avgoEdge: The only true peer. Broadcom wins on scale, deeper IP and packaging, and a dominant Ethernet-switch franchise; Marvell is the clear number two.

differentiation.theyMake: Merchant GPUs and CPUs sold to fight Nvidia head-on
differentiation.avgoEdge: Broadcom sells no merchant accelerators at all; it helps customers build their own. It is a picks-and-shovels supplier, not a chip vendor competing for the same sockets.

differentiation.theyMake: Memory (DRAM and HBM) that sits next to an AI chip
differentiation.avgoEdge: Memory stores data; Broadcom's logic computes and moves it. Micron is commodity-cyclical with prices that swing hard, while Broadcom's custom logic, networking and software are higher-margin and far less commodity.

differentiation.theyMake: CPUs it both designs and manufactures itself
differentiation.avgoEdge: Broadcom is fabless (TSMC builds its chips) and makes no CPUs; it focuses on custom accelerators and networking, avoiding the capital and yield risk of owning fabs.

differentiation.theyMake: Mobile and handset chips for phones
differentiation.avgoEdge: Broadcom largely exited that dependence years ago; the two barely overlap today beyond some connectivity parts.

differentiation.theyMake: Broad analog and embedded chips for cars and industry
differentiation.avgoEdge: Different end-markets and more commodity-like. They are not levered to the AI data-center build that drives Broadcom's growth.

valueDelivered.label: Cheaper, efficient AI compute
valueDelivered.detail: Custom silicon vs merchant GPUs: lower $/token and lower power

valueDelivered.label: Private-cloud software
valueDelivered.detail: VMware VCF as the AI-infra software layer; ARR +19%

leadership.name: Hock E. Tan
leadership.title: President & CEO
leadership.background: President, CEO and director since March 2006; previously CEO of Integrated Circuit Systems and senior finance roles at Commodore, PepsiCo, and GM.

leadership.name: Kirsten M. Spears
leadership.title: CFO & Chief Accounting Officer
leadership.background: Sitting CFO/CAO; retiring effective June 12, 2026, then serving as a consultant.

leadership.name: Amie Thuener
leadership.title: CFO (incoming, eff. June 12, 2026)
leadership.background: Joined Broadcom May 4, 2026 to succeed Spears; previously VP, Corporate Controller and CAO at Alphabet since 2018.

notes: Trading multiples pulled live from yfinance as of the last COMPLETED market close (EV/Rev recomputed from close x shares + net debt; an in-progress session is never treated as a close). Qualitative facts sourced to Broadcom IR/10-K, CNBC, Bloomberg, The Information, and the Q1 FY2026 release/transcript. FQ2 2026 reports 2026-06-03, so all Q2 figures are management guidance, not actuals.

businessOverview: Broadcom (NASDAQ: AVGO) is a ~$2.2T market-cap chip and software maker, now the central arms dealer of the AI buildout.
FY2025 revenue was $63.9B, up ~24% YoY, across two segments.
Semiconductor Solutions: $36.9B (58% of revenue), spanning AI accelerators, networking, broadband, wireless and storage.
Infrastructure Software: $27.0B (42%), led by VMware on multi-year subscriptions; VMware ARR up 19%.
AI is the engine: Q1 FY26 AI revenue was $8.4B (+106% YoY), guided to $10.7B (+140%) in Q2.
It designs custom XPUs (ASICs) with hyperscalers, the merchant alternative to Nvidia's GPUs.
It also owns the AI cluster fabric via Ethernet switching (Tomahawk/Jericho), competing with Nvidia InfiniBand.
AI backlog is ~$73B; CEO Hock Tan guides FY27 AI chip revenue 'significantly' above $100B.
Capital returns are heavy: $10.9B returned in Q1, plus a new $10B buyback authorization.
brief.productModel: Semiconductor Solutions ($36.9B FY2025) monetizes custom ASIC/XPU design wins with hyperscalers plus merchant networking, broadband, wireless and storage chips. Infrastructure Software ($27.0B) monetizes VMware Cloud Foundation and legacy software via multi-year subscriptions after the post-acquisition repricing. The AI slice of semis ($8.4B in Q1 FY26, +106% YoY) is now the dominant growth and valuation driver.

recentNews.headline: Susquehanna lifts Broadcom target to $490 ahead of FQ2
recentNews.whyItMatters: A pre-earnings PT hike (from $450) signals the buy-side expects custom-ASIC and AI-networking upside, but the 'modest FQ3 guide' caveat warns even a revenue beat could sell off if the forward AI guide is merely in-line.

recentNews.headline: Broadcom seen as winner from Blackstone-Google $5B TPU venture
recentNews.whyItMatters: Blackstone pledged ~$5B to launch compute-as-a-service on Google TPUs (which Broadcom co-designs), widening the custom-silicon demand pool beyond core hyperscalers and adding a PE-funded financing channel for AI infrastructure.

recentNews.headline: Citi raises Broadcom price target ahead of FQ2 results
recentNews.whyItMatters: Sequential sell-side target hikes show estimate revisions still trending up into the print, supporting the multiple even at record highs but leaving positioning crowded long.

recentNews.headline: OpenAI-Broadcom custom chip deal hits ~$18B financing snag
recentNews.whyItMatters: Broadcom will reportedly finance only the first phase if Microsoft commits to buy ~40% of the chips, transferring demand risk back to the buyer and reframing the OpenAI relationship from a clean backlog add to a contingent deal.

recentNews.headline: Meta commits ~1 GW of custom chips with Broadcom; Hock Tan to exit Meta board
recentNews.whyItMatters: A gigawatt-scale MTIA commitment diversifies AVGO's custom-silicon revenue beyond Google; Tan stepping off Meta's (not Broadcom's) board cleans up the governance conflict as the relationship scales.

recentNews.headline: Wall Street sees up to ~80% AVGO upside after expanded chip deals
recentNews.whyItMatters: The wide implied-upside range shows the bull case now rests on custom-ASIC TAM expansion rather than legacy semis or VMware, leaving the stock trading on AI-narrative conviction and exposed to multiple compression if order momentum stalls.

recentNews.headline: Broadcom expands chip deals with Google and Anthropic (~3.5 GW)
recentNews.whyItMatters: Anthropic gains ~3.5 GW of Google-TPU compute through 2029 (Mizuho est. ~$21B AVGO AI revenue in 2026, ~$42B in 2027), converting it from a one-off order into a multi-year anchor under the FY27 $100B+ AI narrative.

recentNews.headline: Broadcom beats FQ1; guides FQ2 to ~$22B, AI to $10.7B; new $10B buyback
recentNews.whyItMatters: The $10.7B AI guide (+140%) and ~$73B AI backlog are the quantitative spine of the bull case; the fresh $10B buyback signals FCF confidence and a valuation backstop.

recentNews.headline: Hock Tan: AI chip revenue 'significantly' above $100B in 2027
recentNews.whyItMatters: This is the headline number underpinning every bull DCF and the reason AVGO sustains a premium to the semi group; any update to the $100B+ frame on June 3 will be the dominant post-earnings catalyst.

earningsTakeaways.quarter: FQ1 2026 (ended Feb 1, 2026); most recent reported. FQ2 reports Jun 3, 2026
earningsTakeaways.aiCommentary: AI was the dominant theme: Q1 AI revenue of $8.4B grew 106% YoY (above plan), powered by custom XPUs and AI networking, with the custom-accelerator business up ~140% YoY. Management cited Meta's MTIA shipping now and OpenAI deploying its first-gen XPU in volume in 2027 at over 1 GW. CEO Hock Tan reiterated 'line of sight' to AI chip revenue significantly above $100B in 2027, and guided FQ2 AI revenue to $10.7B (+140% YoY).
earningsTakeaways.demandCommentary: Semiconductor Solutions ($12.5B, +52%) was carried by AI demand while non-AI/core semis (broadband, wireless, industrial) stayed muted. Infrastructure Software ($6.8B, +1%) lapped the VMware integration; VMware ARR rose 19% and TCV bookings were $9.2B. Management framed VMware Cloud Foundation as the software layer for AI infrastructure. Capital returns: $10.9B in Q1 ($3.1B dividends + $7.8B buybacks); board authorized an additional $10B repurchase.

keyRisks: Priced for perfection: a steep EV/Rev premium to Nvidia, Marvell and AMD (see comps) leaves the stock exposed to multiple compression if AI orders slip.
Customer concentration: Google may dual-source TPUs with Marvell, threatening AVGO's largest custom-silicon franchise.
Offtake risk: the OpenAI deal reportedly hinges on Microsoft buying ~40% (~$18B of financing at stake).
Lumpy recognition: AI revenue is backlog-driven with deliveries skewed to 2027, so quarter-to-quarter volatility is high.
VMware: EU licensing scrutiny and subscription-repricing pushback could pressure renewals.
Core (non-AI) semiconductors (broadband, wireless, industrial) remain soft.
Event risk: FQ2 prints June 3, 2026, into a high bar after a sharp run-up.
CFO transition: Amie Thuener takes over June 12, 2026.

comps.name: Broadcom
comps.note: Custom AI silicon plus networking plus software; premium to every listed peer

comps.name: Nvidia
comps.note: Merchant GPU leader; the off-the-shelf chip AVGO's custom XPUs displace

comps.name: Marvell
comps.note: Closest true peer: custom ASICs plus optical/networking; clear number two

comps.name: AMD
comps.note: Merchant GPU/CPU challenger to Nvidia; sells sockets, not custom designs

comps.name: Astera Labs
comps.note: Pure-play AI-rack connectivity (retimers); rich multiple on hypergrowth

comps.name: Micron
comps.note: HBM/DRAM beside AI chips; commodity-cyclical, in an up-cycle (not logic)

comps.name: Qualcomm
comps.note: Mobile/handset chips; minimal overlap with AVGO today, cheapest comp

comps.name: Texas Instruments
comps.note: Broad analog/embedded for industrial and auto; non-AI benchmark

comps.name: NXP Semiconductors
comps.note: Automotive and edge analog; different end-markets, value multiple

comps.name: Intel
comps.note: Designs and fabricates its own CPUs; AVGO is fabless with no CPUs

comps.name: Arm Holdings
comps.note: Licenses CPU IP on royalties; ~88x reflects IP economics, not a chipmaker

slideBullets: AI franchise compounding: Q1 FY26 AI revenue $8.4B (+106% YoY), guided to $10.7B (+140%) in Q2; ~$73B AI backlog.
Custom XPUs for Google, Meta, ByteDance, OpenAI (10 GW) and Anthropic (~3.5 GW), the merchant alternative to Nvidia.
CEO guides FY27 AI chip revenue 'significantly' above $100B; ~$2.2T market cap.
Premium to semis peers on EV/Rev (see live comps); $10.9B returned in Q1 plus a new $10B buyback.
diligenceQuestions: How much of the >$100B FY27 AI target is contracted backlog versus pipeline, and what are the offtake/take-or-pay terms, especially the OpenAI deal reportedly contingent on a ~40% Microsoft commitment?
What is customer concentration within AI/XPU revenue, and how defensible is the Google franchise given reports of Marvell dual-sourcing?
On VMware: what are net renewal and churn trends after the subscription repricing, and what is the exposure to the EU licensing investigation?
